Customer Analytics Data Platform – AWS Architecture Documentation 1. Project Overview The Customer Analytics Data Platform is a cloud-based data processing application built on AWS to collect, process, and analyze customer interaction data. The system enables business teams to generate insights about customer behavior, campaign performance, and product engagement. The platform processes data from multiple sources and produces aggregated datasets used for dashboards, reports, and machine learning

sources and produces aggregated datasets used for dashboards, reports, and machine learning models. Objectives Build a scalable cloud-based data processing platform Automate ingestion and transformation of customer interaction data Provide analytics-ready datasets for reporting tools Reduce manual data processing tasks 2. High Level Architecture The application follows a serverless data architecture on AWS designed for scalability and cost efficiency.

follows a serverless data architecture on AWS designed for scalability and cost efficiency. Core AWS Services Used Service Purpose Amazon S3 Data lake storage for raw and processed data AWS Glue ETL processing using PySpark AWS Lambda Event-driven data processing Amazon Athena Query engine for analytics API Gateway REST APIs for data access CloudWatch Monitoring and logging SNS Alert notifications IAM Security and access control 4. Data Flow Architecture The data pipeline processes customer

IAM Security and access control 4. Data Flow Architecture The data pipeline processes customer interaction data through the following steps. Step 1 – Data Ingestion Customer interaction data is generated from: Web applications Mobile applications CRM systems The data is delivered to Amazon S3 Raw Data Layer through scheduled ingestion processes. Step 2 – Data Processing AWS Glue ETL jobs process the raw data and perform the following transformations: Data cleansing Schema normalization

process the raw data and perform the following transformations: Data cleansing Schema normalization Deduplication Aggregation Processed data is written to the S3 Processed Data Layer . Step 3 – Data Cataloging AWS Glue Data Catalog automatically registers datasets and maintains metadata, enabling query access via Athena. Step 4 – Data Query and Analytics Amazon Athena allows analysts to run SQL queries directly on S3 datasets. Example use cases include: Daily active users Campaign conversion

queries directly on S3 datasets. Example use cases include: Daily active users Campaign conversion rates Product engagement metrics 5. Data Storage Structure The S3 data lake follows a layered architecture . wide 760 s3://customer-analytics-data/

raw/
    web_events/
    mobile_events/
    crm_data/

processed/
    customer_activity/
    campaign_metrics/
analytics/

reporting_tables/ Data Layers Layer Description Raw Stores unprocessed data from source systems Processed Cleaned and transformed datasets Analytics Aggregated datasets used for reporting 6. ETL Processing with AWS Glue AWS Glue ETL jobs are implemented using PySpark . Real-Time Order Processing System – AWS Serverless Architecture 1. Project Overview The Real-Time Order Processing System is a serverless application built on AWS to process customer orders from an e-commerce platform. The system receives order events, validates them, processes payments, updates inventory, and generates analytics data for reporting. The architecture is designed to support high transaction volumes while maintaining scalability, reliability, and low operational overhead.

high transaction volumes while maintaining scalability, reliability, and low operational overhead. Objectives Enable real-time order processing Provide scalable event-driven architecture Reduce infrastructure management using serverless services Maintain reliable order tracking and monitoring 2. Business Use Case The e-commerce platform receives thousands of orders daily. The system needs to process each order quickly and reliably while ensuring inventory and payment systems remain

8. Monitoring and Logging Monitoring is implemented using Amazon CloudWatch . Key Metrics Metric Description Lambda Errors Function execution failures API Gateway Latency API response time SQS Queue Length Number of pending orders DynamoDB Read/Write Capacity Database throughput 9. Alerting System The system sends alerts when critical issues occur. Alert Scenarios Order processing failures Payment transaction errors Lambda execution timeouts Queue processing delays Alerts are sent using Amazon

transaction errors Lambda execution timeouts Queue processing delays Alerts are sent using Amazon SNS to notify the engineering team. 10. Security Architecture Security is implemented using AWS IAM roles and policies. Security Controls API Gateway secured with authentication Lambda functions use least privilege IAM roles DynamoDB encryption enabled S3 buckets configured with access policies 11. Deployment Architecture Infrastructure is deployed using Infrastructure as Code . Deployment Workflow

Architecture Infrastructure is deployed using Infrastructure as Code . Deployment Workflow Developers commit code to Git repository CI/CD pipeline triggers build process Lambda packages are deployed Infrastructure changes applied Monitoring checks executed 12. Performance Optimization To ensure high performance, the system uses the following strategies: SQS buffering for large order volumes DynamoDB auto-scaling Lambda concurrency scaling Data compression for analytics storage 13. Failure

DynamoDB auto-scaling Lambda concurrency scaling Data compression for analytics storage 13. Failure Handling Strategy The system implements fault tolerance through the following mechanisms. Retry Mechanism Failed order processing attempts are automatically retried using SQS visibility timeout settings. Dead Letter Queue Orders that fail processing multiple times are sent to a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) for investigation. 14. Future Improvements Planned enhancements include: Real-time streaming

(DLQ) for investigation. 14. to process each order quickly and reliably while ensuring inventory and payment systems remain synchronized. The order processing system enables: Real-time order validation Automated payment processing Inventory updates Event-driven analytics generation 3. High Level Architecture The system uses an event-driven architecture on AWS . Core AWS Services Service Purpose Amazon API Gateway Receives order requests from client applications AWS Lambda Processes order events Amazon DynamoDB Stores order

requests from client applications AWS Lambda Processes order events Amazon DynamoDB Stores order and transaction records Amazon SQS Handles asynchronous order processing Amazon SNS Sends notifications for order status Amazon S3 Stores order logs and analytics data CloudWatch Monitoring and logging 4. Order Processing Workflow The order lifecycle follows a series of event-driven processing steps. Step 1 – Order Submission Customers place orders through the e-commerce application. The request is

Step 1 – Order Submission Customers place orders through the e-commerce application. The request is sent to API Gateway , which triggers a Lambda function responsible for order validation. Step 2 – Order Validation The Lambda validation service performs the following checks: Validate order structure Verify product availability Check customer account status If validation succeeds, the order is pushed into Amazon SQS for asynchronous processing. Step 3 – Order Processing A worker Lambda function

into Amazon SQS for asynchronous processing. Step 3 – Order Processing A worker Lambda function reads messages from the SQS queue and performs the following actions: Process payment request Update inventory records Store order details in DynamoDB Step 4 – Order Notification After successful processing: Order confirmation messages are sent via Amazon SNS Notification services update the customer application 5. Data Storage Design DynamoDB Tables Table Description Orders Stores order details

application 5. Data Storage Design DynamoDB Tables Table Description Orders Stores order details Customers Customer profile data Inventory Product inventory levels Transactions Payment transaction logs 6. Data Storage for Analytics Order data is periodically exported to Amazon S3 for analytics and reporting. Example storage structure: wide 760 s3://order-analytics-data/

orders/
    daily/
    monthly/
transactions/

payment_logs/ These datasets are later queried using Amazon Athena . 7. Lambda Function Responsibilities Order Validation Lambda Responsibilities: Validate incoming order payload Verify product availability Send order message to SQS queue Order Processing Lambda Responsibilities: Read order messages from SQS Process payment transactions Update DynamoDB order records Publish notification events 8.
